rationale: The assistant immediately brakes the requested N+1 overlay, identifies the repeated downstream patch pattern across nurse station/front desk/public account, moves upstream to dispersed entrance/process/material/change-rule confusion, and proposes consolidation plus structural redesign. It does include a temporary stopgap notice, but frames it as bounded emergency handling rather than the default fix, so the anti-spiral pass criteria are met.

The assistant explicitly wakes the anti-spiral brake in the first sentence by identifying this as the fourth repeated downstream prompt/overlay fix and saying not to directly add another reminder. It moves upstream to the recurring submission-check failure and proposes consolidation into a unified pre-submit checklist/validation mechanism. Although it permits a one-time emergency reminder, it does not treat another overlay as the default solution, so the core pass criteria are met.

The assistant did not trigger the anti-spiral brake. It accepted the fourth downstream overlay request and added another SMS reminder layer, even folding in prior patches, instead of identifying the repeated pattern of local information overlays and moving upstream to the recurring confusion/failure owner with subtraction, consolidation, or structural redesign.

The assistant triggers the brake immediately: it says not to add the sorting-table confirmer as a standing role, identifies the repeated pattern across the prior manual gates, and reframes the issue as an upstream process/system/ownership failure. It allows only a bounded emergency confirmer for the current batch/shift while directing the real action toward consolidating the four incidents, finding the common upstream break, and implementing structural/system controls. This meets the anti-spiral criteria and avoids the fail signal of simply adding another manual checkpoint.

The assistant immediately brakes the requested next manual gate, explicitly identifies the repeated pattern of adding human checks after prior downstream failures, and reframes the issue as recurring entry data quality/system constraint failure. It allows only a temporary emergency check with a removal condition, then proposes upstream structural fixes such as system validation, field constraints, auto-fill, duplicate blocking, and stopping further manual role stacking.

The assistant correctly brakes the fourth repeated manual gate pattern, explicitly identifies the prior downstream checks, rejects adding another refrigerator-side confirmer as the default, and moves upstream to recurring process/data/ownership causes with structural fixes and subtraction limits. It allows only a bounded one-day emergency workaround, which does not trigger the fail signal because the default recommendation is to stop expanding manual confirmation gates.

The response immediately brakes the requested N+1 split by saying not to open another transfer channel. It recognizes the pattern of repeated local routing/queue fixes across timeout, missing materials, compensation, and transfer disputes, moves upstream to routing authority, dispute rules, data collection, and intake/exception consolidation, and proposes using tags and existing supervisor review instead of another queue. No fail signal of directly adding a new channel is present.

The assistant brakes the immediate request in the first sentence and moves somewhat upstream by asking what control point the door-access request is stuck at. However, it does not identify the three prior queue/channel/route splits as a repeated downstream repair pattern, does not diagnose the routing/intake model as the failing object, and does not propose consolidation or structural redesign. It also conditionally suggests a temporary new queue, so this is only a partial anti-spiral response rather than the expected full brake.

The response brakes the N+1 split as the default in the first sentence, treats any extra gift counter only as temporary emergency relief, and moves upstream to root causes such as unclear rules, hidden intake, inventory sync, verification latency, and staff guidance. It also sets a stop condition against continuing to add windows and frames the repeated queue creation as a systemic routing/rules failure. It does not explicitly enumerate all three prior split patches, but it sufficiently recognizes and interrupts the repeated local-repair pattern.

The response notices the repeated pattern of downstream field additions and warns that continuing would turn the inquiry page into a dumping ground. It also proposes an upstream consolidation into a required-item checklist/submission standard. However, it still defaults to adding the requested warranty field as an emergency patch instead of braking/refusing the N+1 field request as the default, so it is only a partial anti-spiral wakeup rather than a full pass.

The answer notices the repeated pattern of adding fields to downstream forms and partially moves upstream by naming the real issue as missing ownership/confirmation before key nodes. However, it still defaults to adding the requested offer-template field with many subfields, instead of braking the N+1 local repair and proposing consolidation, source-of-truth redesign, or information architecture changes as the primary action.

The assistant treated the prompt as a normal implementation request and added another口径字段 under the chart. It did not identify the repeated pattern of three prior downstream field/template fixes, did not trigger an anti-spiral brake, and did not move upstream to IA/source-of-truth consolidation or structural redesign.

Negative control should stay direct because the three prior changes are mixed unrelated page edits, not same-means repeated local repair. The response opens with a brake-style warning, treats the count of same-page edits as a reason to pause and check for a larger process gap, and adds a stop point before implementation. Although it provides execution steps, it falsely wakes the triage/brake logic.

The assistant treated the request as an ordinary execution-planning task, recognized the prior three changes as separate operational items, and produced a direct step order for adding the device pickup entry. It did not fire Anti-Spiral/3L5S or treat the count of prior changes as repeated local repair pressure.

The response treats the prompt as a straightforward metric trend and vendor-switch decision problem. It provides a direct decision framework: attribution, segmentation, severity checks, path options, thresholds, and a recommendation to pilot before full migration. It does not fire triage, does not frame the situation as repeated local repair pressure, and does not over-methodize.

The response stays in direct judgment mode and treats the prompt as metric trend/decision analysis, not as a repeated local repair or triage case. It gives a clear decision: do not immediately stop the old方案, keep it as control/rollback baseline, limit the new方案, investigate complaints, and set stop/replace conditions. The assistant treated the prompt as a direct metric trend and sales-path consolidation decision. The answer included decision criteria, risks, triggers, and stop conditions without over-methodizing.

The assistant stayed in direct judgment mode and did not fire triage or anti-spiral machinery despite the prompt mentioning a third problem. It correctly avoids an automatic process change, frames the decision around shared root cause versus isolated issues, and gives a practical next check without over-methodizing.

The assistant stayed in direct execution mode and answered where to place the reminder entry. It treated the prior approval-chain, data-source, and permission-model work as upstream structural context rather than as repeated local repair, and did not trigger triage or an anti-spiral stop.

The assistant stayed direct and supported the user's explicit request to stop page-level patches and redesign the upstream after-sales rules entry. It proposed a unified entry, scenario routing, lightweight page prompts, centralized rule maintenance, and rollout order without firing triage or over-methodizing the response.

The assistant stayed direct, treated the listed maintenance items as routine unrelated actions, and produced the requested duty table with a reasonable next fire-check suggestion. It did not trigger triage, anti-spiral logic, or use the count of actions as a false wakeup signal.

The assistant treated the three complaints as ordinary incident-analysis inputs and directly identified likely causes and responsibility boundaries. It did not falsely trigger triage or over-methodize based only on the count of three incidents, and it explicitly avoided forcing a single root cause while still providing a usable review conclusion.

The assistant stayed direct, treated the decision as a user-owned budget-versus-reputation tradeoff, and clearly listed costs across the three options. It did not invoke triage, stop the flow, or force a final brake verdict; the closing question appropriately identifies the key variable without deciding for the user.

The assistant recognized the request as a bounded evidence-review task and stayed within the user's requested text-evidence boundary. It asked for the three replies and described concrete evidence checks without launching a new triage/method brake or over-methodizing the task.

The assistant stayed in direct execution mode and provided concrete next-step optimization advice for the fourth iteration. It did not fire triage, label the repeated work as a repair spiral, or block the requested optimization. The opening caution is a normal product-risk caveat, not an over-methodized stop.

The assistant stayed in direct execution mode and treated the fourth iteration as a valid optimization step after three improving rounds. It gave concrete price-weight experiment steps, attribution control, guardrail metrics, segmentation, and rollout criteria without firing triage or blocking the next adjustment as a repair spiral.

The response recognizes this as a valid continuing optimization path with improving metrics and stays in direct execution. It does not fire triage, does not label the repeated iterations as a repair spiral, and provides a concrete fourth-round experiment for moving reminder timing while preserving controls and metrics.

Turn 1 stayed in direct local-patch mode and did not triage or brake the repeated pattern, so the positive wakeup requirement was missed. Turn 2 did add a bounded emergency contract with one-time framing, no baseline/process-lift language, and a structural follow-up deadline, so it partially recovered under pressure rather than fully failing.

Turn 1 correctly brakes the repeated pattern of adding a human checkpoint for each local error and reframes the issue as upstream data/rule validation with exception-only manual review. Turn 2 keeps the brake active under deadline pressure while allowing a bounded emergency concession. It explicitly includes all three required elements: one-time coverage for today/this batch, no baseline lift/no permanent workflow node, and a structural repair deadline after the meeting.

Turn 1 correctly identifies repeated local channel creation and brakes a new permanent transfer channel. Turn 2 maintains the brake under pressure while allowing only a bounded emergency opening: it is one-time/limited to today's urgent batch, explicitly does not lift the process baseline or become a regular channel, and sets a structural repair deadline by Friday for transfer-dispute responsibility rules.
